EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As warm air headed north ahead of low pressure moving into the Ohio Valley freezing rain quickly developed across much of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Untreated roadways quickly accumulated up to one tenth of an inch of ice making travel nearly impossible between 6 AM and 10 AM. Major interstates in and around Pittsburgh, including the Pennsylvania Turnpike were closed for more than 2 hours until roads could be treated and surface temperatures warmed above freezing. 911 centers across the area received thousands of call due to minor accidents. 2 more serious accidents resulted in 2 deaths, one in Beaver county, PA and one in Westmoreland county, PA.
A thin line of storms produced isolated severe weather over portions of Ohio during the late afternoon and early evening hours of October 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were reported down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong center of low pressure tracked out of the plains states to the Great Lakes region. Ahead of this low in the Ohio Valley, southwest winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 and 60 mph were common throughout the day. These strong winds peaked in the early afternoon with the passage of a cold front, and diminished later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs were blown down onto power lines in the Richwood area.

A strong pressure gradient behind a cold front produced damaging winds across all of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, eastern Ohio, and Garrett county Maryland. Sustained winds were from 30 to 40 MPH with gusts over 60 MPH. The strongest reported wind gusts were 68 MPH in Franklin, PA and 66 MPH in Eighty Four, PA. Trees and power lines were reported down across the region, with more than 50,000 homes without power late afternoon on the 9th at the peak of the winds.

Arctic air moved over the Upper Ohio Valley with a large dome of High pressure. Morning lows across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. The lowest low temperatures ranged from 10 below zero at Pittsburgh to 22 below zero at Accident, Maryland. Low temperatures were generally 10 to 15 degrees below zero elsewhere.

A strong storm system brought heavy snow to portions of western Pennsylvania and eastern Ohio with snow accumulations of 7 to 10 inches by late afternoon on January 10th. Mixed rain and snow fell across portions of southwest Pennsylvania, southeast Ohio, northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland with light snow accumulations.

A deepening area of low pressure tracked from Tennessee northeast into western Pennsylvania late January 27th into January 28th. A swath of heavy snow fell to the northwest of this track, which affected portions of northwest Ohio. Total snow accumulations ranged between 5 and 9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, moderate to heavy at times, developed during the afternoon hours on January 27th and continued through the morning hours of January 28th. Total snow accumulations of 6 to 9 inches were reported across the county, with a report of 7.8 inches at Van Wert. There were reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region. Most schools were closed on January 28th due to the heavy snowfall.

A low pressure system tracked east-northeast through the Ohio Valley late January 9th into January 10th. A narrow band of heavy snow fell on the northern fringe of this storm system across northwest Ohio, with total snowfall amounts generally ranging between 5 and 9 inches.

A cold snap claimed the life of a woman in Butler county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman wandered from her nursing home in Fairfield and succumbed to hypothermia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked east through the Ohio Valley during the afternoon. This set up a boundary extending from just west of Richmond, Indiana, through eastern Cincinnati, to Maysville, Kentucky. A mid level disturbance created a very strong source of localized lift, which produced snowfall rates exceeding two inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow fell in Oxford, with four and a half inches in Tylersville and three and a half inches in Hamilton.

A frontal boundary was stalled over the Tennessee Valley for the early part of the week. Upper level disturbances crossed through the Ohio Valley during this time and accumulating snowfall began on Tuesday. Warmer air aloft on Tuesday afternoon brought a significant amount of freezing rain to areas south of I-70 and especially near the Ohio River.||Significant snowfall occurred on the back side of this system with snowfall rates on Wednesday morning approaching two inches per hour along the I-71 corridor between Cincinnati and Columbus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over five inches of snow was measured at the county garage in West Union.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure tracked across the Great Lakes, dragging along a strong cold front. Behind the front sustained winds were frequently in the 35 to 45 mph range with gusts of between 55 and as high as 70 mph in some areas. This caused scattered to numerous trees and power line damage along with some structure damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines down were noted by county officals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved north across West Virginia to western Pennsylvania producing a heavy mix of precipitation. Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches across northwest Pennsylvania on the night of the 27Th before changing to freezing rain the morning of the 28Th, then back to snow in the afternoon with another 1 to 2 inches. Ice accumulations in Pittsburgh and areas north were from a quarter to one half inch. For southeast Ohio, northern West Virgina, and Garrett county Maryland mixed precipitation changed to freezing rain during the evening of the 27Th with ice quickly accumulating to one half inch before changing to plain overnight. The rain changed back to snow during the afternoon of the 28Th with 1 to 2 inches of snow accumulating.

A deepening area of low pressure tracked from Tennessee northeast into western Pennsylvania late January 27th into January 28th. A swath of heavy snow fell to the northwest of this track, which affected portions of northwest Ohio. Total snow accumulations ranged between 5 and 9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, moderate to heavy at times, developed during the afternoon hours on January 27th and continued through the morning hours of January 28th. Total snow accumulations of 5 to 8 inches were reported across the county, with a report of 6 inches at Napoleon. There were reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region. Most schools were closed on January 28th due to the heavy snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed over northeast Mercer and northwest Auglaize Counties, producing a weak tornado and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vinyl siding was removed off a house along State Route 117.  Large tree limbs were also downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed over northeast Mercer and northwest Auglaize Counties, producing a weak tornado and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water rushing rapidly over State Route 117.  Several other roads flooded and closed across northeast Mercer County.  A few drainage ditches were overflowing as well.

An east-west frontal boundary stretched across central and northern Indiana with an unstable and moderately sheared environment in place. Numerous thunderstorms developed with several small bowing segments moving out of northern Indiana into northwestern Ohio, producing wind damage as well as some isolated large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported a power pole down 2 miles southeast of Van Wert.

Numerous thunderstorms developed with several small bowing segments moving out of northern Indiana into northwestern Ohio, producing wind damage as well as some isolated large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials surveyed a concentrated area of damage, appearing to be the result of a micro-burst. Within a roughly one mile square area, one barn roof was blown off, three aluminum and one wooden barn door were damaged, several 100 year old trees were blown down by the winds. Also, around 20 structures suffered mainly shingle damage. Damage is estimated at $75,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving line of storms moved out of northern Indiana and into northwestern Ohio. While the storms had weakened prior to their arrival in Ohio, training of a few of the storms caused areas of flooding and flash flooding across portions of southern Williams county and northern Defiance county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported flooding across the northern part of the county. Several locations along State Road 249 were affected between Lake Road, west of Farmer, to near US 127, east of Farmer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of thunderstorms moved through during the afternoon hours sparking straight line damaging winds, large hail, flash flooding, and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado initially touched down near a grocery store near State Route 772 and Valley Road in Summithill where several trees were knocked down and uprooted.  There were several intermittent touch downs between Summithill and Denver.  To the southeast of Summithill, along Schaffer Road, approximately six homes and two mobile homes were damaged and numerous trees were snapped and uprooted.  An eye-witness on Schaffer Road reported a rope tornado which had several brief touch downs.  Further southeast in the Denver area, a home received substantial damage with a loss of the roof and some walls down.  Portions of the roof were thrown 700 yards and wrapped around trees.  There was also widespread crop damage in the area.  Two barns off of Denver Road were completely destroyed and uprooted trees were observed intermittently along this path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of thunderstorms moved through during the afternoon hours sparking straight line damaging winds, large hail, flash flooding, and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage indicates that the tornado touched down briefly in two locations.  The initial touch down occurred near Loop Road where an estimated 15 trees were uprooted and knocked down.  The tornado then briefly lifted before touching down again in a cemetery off of Bailey Chapel Road.  There, it uprooted and knocked down approximately 10 more trees, and lifted a nearby trampoline over a fence.

A strong center of low pressure tracked out of the plains states to the Great Lakes region. Ahead of this low in the Ohio Valley, southwest winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 and 60 mph were common throughout the day. These strong winds peaked in the early afternoon with the passage of a cold front, and diminished later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large business sign was blown apart on State Route 48 in Englewood. Newspaper reports in the New Lebanon area showed a large tree was blown down and several power poles that were damaged. The ASOS at KDAY Dayton international airport recorded a 56 mph gust at 13:19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure moved northeast across the central Great Lakes on December 9th.  A strong cold front stretching south from this low swept east across northern Ohio during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 9th.  Damaging high winds occurred behind this cold front.  Many trees, utility poles and power lines were reported down.  Peak wind gusts at most locations occurred late in the afternoon.  Also, a line of showers developed ahead of the front as it moved across Northeast Ohio late in the morning on the 9th.  A few of the stronger showers produced wind gusts in excess of 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Ashland County around midday on December 9th.  Southwest winds behind the front rapidly increased with gusts in excess of 45 mph by early afternoon.  Gusts continued to increase the remainder of the afternoon with peak gusts of around 60 mph reported during the late afternoon hours.  Winds continued to gust to as much as 50 mph through 10 pm. The high winds downed a few trees and utility poles in Ashland County.  Scattered power outages were reported.  Some homes and buildings in the county sustained minor damage.  Most of this was from lost siding or roofing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure moved northeast across the central Great Lakes on December 9th.  A strong cold front stretching south from this low swept east across northern Ohio during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 9th.  Damaging high winds occurred behind this cold front.  Many trees, utility poles and power lines were reported down.  Peak wind gusts at most locations occurred late in the afternoon.  Also, a line of showers developed ahead of the front as it moved across Northeast Ohio late in the morning on the 9th.  A few of the stronger showers produced wind gusts in excess of 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Richland County just before noon on December 9th.  Southwest winds behind the front rapidly increased with gusts in excess of 45 mph by early afternoon.  Gusts continued to increase the remainder of the afternoon with peak gusts of around 60 mph reported during the late afternoon hours.  A 55 mph gust was measured at Mansfield Lahm Airport at 3:18 pm.  Winds continued to gust to as much as 50 mph through 10 pm. The high winds downed a few trees and utility poles in Richland County.  Scattered power outages were reported.  Some homes and buildings in the county sustained minor damage.  Most of this was from lost siding or roofing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong center of low pressure tracked out of the plains states to the Great Lakes region. Ahead of this low in the Ohio Valley, southwest winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 and 60 mph were common throughout the day. These strong winds peaked in the early afternoon with the passage of a cold front, and diminished later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening area of low pressure tracked from Tennessee northeast into western Pennsylvania late January 27th into January 28th. A swath of heavy snow fell to the northwest of this track, which affected portions of northwest Ohio. Total snow accumulations ranged between 5 and 9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, moderate to heavy at times, developed during the afternoon hours on January 27th and continued through the morning hours of January 28th. Total snow accumulations of 5 to 8 inches were reported across the county. There were reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region. Most schools were closed on January 28th due to the heavy snowfall.

An arctic cold front moved across the region on December 9th.  Cold westerly winds blowing across Lake Erie behind this front caused flurries and light snow showers to develop by the late evening hours of the 9th.  This activity continued into the 10th with little if any accumulation reported through late afternoon.  The lake effect activity began to increase during the evening of the 10th as even colder air infiltrated the region.  Moderate to heavy snow began during the early morning hours of December 11th and finally tapered off late in the day.  Westerly winds gusted to as much as 30 mph for much of this event.  This caused considerable blowing and drifting.  A few locations in Ashtabula County saw as much as ten inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold westerly winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop on December 10th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were reported in Ashtabula County during the evening hours of the 10th and also during the morning and early afternoon hours of the 11th.  Visibilities at times were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of around an inch per hour.  The snow showers tapered to flurries just before sunset on the 11th.  Snowfall totals across the northern third of Ashtabula County ranged from 6 to 10 inches.  Lesser amounts were reported further south in the county.   Westerly winds gusted to as much as 30 mph for much of this event.  This caused considerable blowing and drifting which hampered travel.   Several accidents were reported.

Gusts continued to increase the remainder of the afternoon with peak gusts of around 60 mph reported during the late afternoon hours.  Winds continued to gust to as much as 50 mph through 10 pm.  The high winds downed a few trees and utility poles in Huron County.  Scattered power outages were reported.  Some homes and buildings in the county sustained minor damage.  Most of this was from lost siding or roofing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure moved northeast across the central Great Lakes on December 9th.  A strong cold front stretching south from this low swept east across northern Ohio during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 9th.  Damaging high winds occurred behind this cold front.  Many trees, utility poles and power lines were reported down.  Peak wind gusts at most locations occurred late in the afternoon.  Also, a line of showers developed ahead of the front as it moved across Northeast Ohio late in the morning on the 9th.  A few of the stronger showers produced wind gusts in excess of 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Lorain County just before noon on December 9th.  Southwest winds behind the front rapidly increased with gusts in excess of 45 mph by early afternoon.  Gusts continued to increase the remainder of the afternoon with peak gusts of around 60 mph reported during the late afternoon hours.  Winds continued to gust to as much as 50 mph through 10 pm.  A 58 mph wind gust was measured at the Lorain County Airport near Elyria at 4:31 pm. The high winds downed a few trees and utility poles in Lorain County.  Scattered power outages were reported.  Some homes and buildings in the county sustained minor damage.  Most of this was from lost siding or roofing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across the region on December 9th.  Cold westerly winds blowing across Lake Erie behind this front caused flurries and light snow showers to develop by the late evening hours of the 9th.  This activity continued into the 10th with little if any accumulation reported through late afternoon.  The lake effect activity began to increase during the evening of the 10th as even colder air infiltrated the region.  Moderate to heavy snow began during the early morning hours of December 11th and finally tapered off late in the day.  Westerly winds gusted to as much as 30 mph for much of this event.  This caused considerable blowing and drifting.  A few locations in Ashtabula County saw as much as ten inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold westerly winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop on December 10th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were reported in Ashtabula County during the evening hours of the 10th and also during the morning and early afternoon hours of the 11th.  Visibilities at times were less than a half mile with snowfall rates of around an inch per hour.  The snow showers tapered to flurries just before sunset on the 11th.  Snowfall totals across the northern third of Ashtabula County ranged from 6 to 10 inches.  A peak total of 11.7 inches was reported by a spotter in the City of Ashtabula.  Lesser amounts were reported further south in the county.   Westerly winds gusted to as much as 30 mph for much of this event.  This caused considerable blowing and drifting which hampered travel.   Several accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong center of low pressure tracked out of the plains states to the Great Lakes region. Ahead of this low in the Ohio Valley, southwest winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 and 60 mph were common throughout the day. These strong winds peaked in the early afternoon with the passage of a cold front, and diminished later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately two dozen trees were reported down across Portsmouth, leaving 1200 people without power. The gust was measured at 12:41.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure located over eastern Canada was nearly stationary during this time. This feature combined with warm and humid air at the surface caused showers and thunderstorms to develop by mid afternoon. Thunderstorms continued across the entire region during the evening hours, with many becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was observed.

This feature combined with warm and humid air at the surface caused showers and thunderstorms to develop by mid afternoon. Thunderstorms continued across the entire region during the evening hours, with many becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and large limbs were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked across the region along a cold front during the evening hours of June 1st. The cold front stretched from the Northern Great Lakes to the southern Plains. Some of the thunderstorms became severe during the evening hours. The activity pushed off to the east and diminished shortly before midnight on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossed the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 18th. Low pressure tracked up the east coast, with the westward extent of its moisture reaching into Ohio. As the upper low crossed, temperatures cooled and rain changed to snow, accumulating to 2 to 4 inches for the Scioto River Valley and portions of northern Kentucky. Other areas in Ohio got 1 to 2 inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county garage in Piketon measured 4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across northeastern Ohio during the evening hours of December 27th.  Cold northwest winds behind this front caused lake effect snow showers to develop late in the evening.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were reported from late on the 27th through late morning on the 29th.  The first of these periods occurred during the early morning hours of the 28th with a second period during the afternoon and evening hours of the 28th.  A brief period of heavy snow was also reported on the morning of the 29th.  Light snow or flurries occurred between the periods of heavier snow.  The snow ended completely around midday on the 29th.  6 to 10 inches of accumulation was reported across portions of the Northeast Ohio snowbelt.  Many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the evening hours of December 27th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow then continued through midday on December 29th.  Heavy snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 28th, during the afternoon and evening of the 28th and also on the morning of the 29th.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  Light snow or flurries were reported between the periods of heavier snow.  Snowfall accumulations across Geauga County ranged from 6 to 10 inches during this event.  A maximum of 10.0 inches was measured in Russell Township with 9.9 inches in Hambden Township.  A few accidents were reported.

A brief period of heavy snow was also reported on the morning of the 29th.  Light snow or flurries occurred between the periods of heavier snow.  The snow ended completely around midday on the 29th.  6 to 10 inches of accumulation was reported across portions of the Northeast Ohio snowbelt.  Many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the evening hours of December 27th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow then continued through midday on December 29th.  Heavy snow occurred during the early morning hours of the 28th, during the afternoon and evening of the 28th and also on the morning of the 29th.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  Light snow or flurries were reported between the periods of heavier snow.  Snowfall accumulations across inland Ashtabula County ranged from 6 to 9 inches during this event.  A maximum of 9.5 inches was measured at Pierpont.  A few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved up the Ohio River Valley and overspread southeast Ohio shortly after midnight on Tuesday the 27th.  The snow quickly accumulated 4 to 6 inches across Jackson, Lawrence, Gallia, and Meigs by mid morning, with 2 to 4 inches common further north.  ||The snow became lighter by early afternoon.  The snow transitioned to freezing rain from south to north, reaching Perry County by 1900E on the 27th. The intensity of the freezing rain increased for the evening hours, with temperatures still in the upper 20s.  Ice accumulations of a quarter to a half inch were widespread.  The intensity of the freezing rain decreased during the predawn hours of the 28th.  Once the low pressure center lifted northeast through West Virginia, the storm ended as a quick burst of snow, during the daylight hours.  This added a coating of snow on top of the ice.||Damage to structures was minimal. The main problem was the loss of electricity due to tree limbs falling across power lines.  Over 25,000 customers had no power.  Some rural areas were without electricity for 4 days.  One electric company official said it was the worst ice storm in his area in over 15 years.  A few shelters were open for people without heat, but only a handfull of residents stayed.  Law enforcement and fire departments conducted wellness checks and assisted where needed.

A strong area of low pressure moved north across West Virginia to western Pennsylvania producing a heavy mix of precipitation. Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches across northwest Pennsylvania on the night of the 27Th before changing to freezing rain the morning of the 28Th, then back to snow in the afternoon with another 1 to 2 inches. Ice accumulations in Pittsburgh and areas north were from a quarter to one half inch. For southeast Ohio, northern West Virgina, and Garrett county Maryland mixed precipitation changed to freezing rain during the evening of the 27Th with ice quickly accumulating to one half inch before changing to plain overnight. The rain changed back to snow during the afternoon of the 28Th with 1 to 2 inches of snow accumulating.

A strong area of arctic high pressure moved from the Northern Plains on on January 15th to the Upper Ohio Valley by late in the day on January 16th.  The high eventually shifted east of the area on the 17th.  Very cold temperatures accompanied this high.  The coldest temperatures since January 1994 were recorded at most observing sites.  Gusty southwest to west winds made it feel even colder with wind chills colder than minus 25 reported from the evening of the 15th through the afternoon hours of the 16th.  Winds eventually lessened early on the 17th which allowed temperatures to again plummet to 10 below or colder for the third morning in a row at many locations.  Cooperative observers in Knox County reported lows of -28 and -29 on the 17th.  Most of the schools in Northern Ohio were closed on both the 15th and 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold temperatures were reported in Mahoning County on January 15th through the 17th.  Temperatures were the coldest since January of 1994.  Gusty southwest to west winds accompanied the cold temperatures and created wind chills colder than minus 25 for several hours on January 16th.  Morning lows the 15th, 16th and 17th were well below zero across the entire county.  Morning lows on the 16th and 17th were colder than minus 10 at most locations.  All of the schools in the county were closed on the 15th and 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved east through the Ohio Valley and took a path along the Ohio River. Very cold air was in place from an earlier cold front, and snow overspread the region along and north of the track of the surface low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow was measured in Kenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure tracked across the Great Lakes, dragging along a strong cold front. Behind the front sustained winds were frequently in the 35 to 45 mph range with gusts of between 55 and as high as 70 mph in some areas. This caused scattered to numerous trees and power line damage along with some structure damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines down were noted by county officals. A mesonet site in Paulding recorded a 70 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed to the north of Lake Erie on January 18th.  A cold front extending south from the low swept east across Northeast Ohio by daybreak on the 18th.  Cold westerly winds behind the front caused lake effect snow bands to quickly develop.  The snow showers tapered off during the evening hours after dumping 6 to 8 inches of snow on portions of Lake and Ashtabula Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Ashtabula County during the early morning hours of January 18th.  Cold westerly winds behind this front caused lake effect snow showers to quickly develop.  The snow showers tapered off during the evening hours after dumping 6 to 8 inches of snow on portions of Ashtabula County.  A maximum of 8.5 inches was measured three miles west of Dorset with 8.3 inches reported at Pierpont.  A few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of arctic high pressure moved from the Northern Plains on on January 15th to the Upper Ohio Valley by late in the day on January 16th.  The high eventually shifted east of the area on the 17th.  Very cold temperatures accompanied this high.  The coldest temperatures since January 1994 were recorded at most observing sites.  Gusty southwest to west winds made it feel even colder with wind chills colder than minus 25 reported from the evening of the 15th through the afternoon hours of the 16th.  Winds eventually lessened early on the 17th which allowed temperatures to again plummet to 10 below or colder for the third morning in a row at many locations.  Cooperative observers in Knox County reported lows of -28 and -29 on the 17th.  Most of the schools in Northern Ohio were closed on both the 15th and 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold temperatures were reported in Ashland County on January 15th through the 17th.  Temperatures were the coldest since January of 1994.  Gusty southwest to west winds accompanied the cold temperatures and created wind chills colder than minus 30 for several hours on January 16th.  Morning lows were generally minus 10 or colder on the 15th, 16th and 17th.  Temperatures struggled to get above zero for highs on the 16th.  All of the schools in the county were closed on the 15th and 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center tracked from Missouri to Michigan.  Meanwhile, its associated upper level trough pushed a cold front through southeast Ohio just before sunset.  A fast moving band of rain, along and immediately ahead of the front, featured a narrow line of embedded showers.  These convective showers helped mix down the winds that were located at 4 to 6 thousand feet above the ground.  Surface wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were common.||Later that night, wind gusts near 60 mph occurred, as the colder air poured in.||Power outages were common throughout southeast Ohio, as tree branches fell onto power lines.  One major utility company reported the electricity remained out until late on the 13th or early on the 14th for some of its customers in southeast Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked eastward from the Mississippi Valley, and moved through the Ohio Valley on the 10th. With the low tracking south of the region colder air was able to remain in place allowing for mainly snow. Snow developed during the late evening hours of the 9th across southwest portions of the forecast area and slowly progressed east through the overnight hours. The snow didn't reach far northeastern Ohio until the early morning hours of the 10th. The snow was heavy at times during the morning hours, then tapered off from west to east through mid afternoon. Accumulations ranged mainly between 6 and 12 inches with highest amounts over northwest Ohio, and the least across central Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the afternoon hours of the 9th and continued through the early evening hours of the 10th. Across northern portions of the county, some lake effect snow showers persisted into the overnight hours. The snow was heaviest at times throughout both the morning and afternoon of the 10th. Precipitation changed from snow to a mix of sleet and freezing rain for a brief time in the afternoon on the 10th, but overall was not enough to limit snowfall accumulations significantly. Snow totals across the area ranged from 6.0 to 12.0 inches, with the highest accumulations over northern parts of the county. Trained spotters measured 12.0 inches in Hiram and 9.5 inches in Kent. Many accidents were reported across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow quickly developed during the afternoon of the 9th and visibilities dropped below 3 miles. The snow persisted through the overnight hours into the 10th, and didn't come to an end until late that evening. There was a brief period of freezing rain late on the 10th, then precipitation ended and pushed out of the area. Snowfall amounts varied between 6.0 and 12.0 inches. Trained spotters in the area measured 9.0 inches in Austintown and 11.0 inches in Boardman. Numerous accidents were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked northeast from the Tennessee Valley during the evening hours of the 27th. This area of low pressure continued northeast and moved through the Ohio River Valley, and then into western Pennsylvania during the early afternoon of the 28th. The low pressure intensified as it entered western New York State. Snow began across the region during the late afternoon hours of the 27th, and continued through the evening of the 28th. Warmer air aloft was able to effect some areas by changing precipitation over to sleet and freezing rain for a short period. Many locations across northern Ohio experienced at snowfall amounts between 8.0 and 12.0 inches.  Gusty westerly winds accompanied the snow and caused extensive blowing and drifting.  Travel was severely hampered during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the afternoon hours of the 27th, and continued through the overnight and morning hours of the 28th. The snow finally tapered off during the afternoon hours. The snow was moderate to occasionally heavy during the morning of the 28th with visibilities dropping to a half mile or less at times. Snowfall totals ranged between 7.0 and 10.0 inches across the area. Trained spotters measured 9.0 inches in Wellington, 8.9 inches in North Ridgeville, 8.8 inches in Oberlin, and 7.6 inches in Sheffield Lake. West to northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph with occasional gusts in excess of 30 mph accompanied the snow. Widespread blowing and drifting was reported with drifts in some areas a couple feet deep.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

This area of low pressure continued northeast and moved through the Ohio River Valley, and then into western Pennsylvania during the early afternoon of the 28th. Warmer air aloft was able to effect some areas by changing precipitation over to sleet and freezing rain for a short period. Many locations across northern Ohio experienced at snowfall amounts between 8.0 and 12.0 inches.  Gusty westerly winds accompanied the snow and caused extensive blowing and drifting. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the afternoon hours of the 27th, and continued overnight and through the morning of the 28th. The snow finally tapered off during the afternoon of the 28th. However, during the early morning hours, the snow changed over to freezing rain and lasted through the mid morning. As the low pressure pulled away from the area, colder air moved back into the area changing the freezing rain back to snow before ending. While snowfall accumulations were only in the 4.0 to 8.0 inch range, freezing rain accumulated to around a quarter of an inch in some locations. Fortunately there were no major problems such as widespread power outages or downed trees with the freezing rain, however, many accidents were reported. Gusty westerly winds accompanied the precipitation and caused some blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of arctic high pressure moved from the Northern Plains on on January 15th to the Upper Ohio Valley by late in the day on January 16th.  The high eventually shifted east of the area on the 17th.  Very cold temperatures accompanied this high.  The coldest temperatures since January 1994 were recorded at most observing sites.  Gusty southwest to west winds made it feel even colder with wind chills colder than minus 25 reported from the evening of the 15th through the afternoon hours of the 16th.  Winds eventually lessened early on the 17th which allowed temperatures to again plummet to 10 below or colder for the third morning in a row at many locations.  Cooperative observers in Knox County reported lows of -28 and -29 on the 17th.  Most of the schools in Northern Ohio were closed on both the 15th and 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold temperatures were reported in northern Ashtabula County on January 15th through the 17th.  Temperatures were the coldest since January of 1994.  Gusty southwest to west winds accompanied the cold temperatures and created wind chills colder than minus 20 for several hours on January 16th.  Morning lows the 15th, 16th and 17th were below zero across the entire county.  Most of the schools in the county were closed on the 15th and 16th.

All of the schools in the county were closed on the 15th and 16th.

Gusty westerly winds accompanied the precipitation and caused some blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked northeast from the Tennessee Valley during the evening hours of the 27th. This area of low pressure continued northeast and moved through the Ohio River Valley, and then into western Pennsylvania during the early afternoon of the 28th. Many locations across northern Ohio experienced at snowfall amounts between 8.0 and 12.0 inches.  Gusty westerly winds accompanied the snow and caused extensive blowing and drifting.  Travel was severely hampered during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the afternoon hours of the 27th, and continued through the overnight and morning of the 28th. The snow quickly tapered off during the afternoon. Snow was moderate to heavy at times during the morning of the 28th. Across far southern parts of the county the snow mixed with some sleet and freezing rain for a brief period during the late night and early morning hours. Despite the mixed precipitation, the snow was still able to accumulate to between 6.0 and 10.0 inches across the area. The highway department in the county measured 9.0 inches of snow by the end of the event. Westerly winds of 15 to 25 mph with occasional gusts in excess of 30 mph accompanied the precipitation. Widespread blowing and drifting was reported with drifts in some areas a couple feet deep.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

As the low pressure pulled away from the area, colder air moved back into the area and changed any of the mixed precipitation back to snow. Snowfall accumulations were in the 6.0 to 10.0 inch range, but at least a quarter inch of ice was reported in the area during the morning hours. A trained spotter in Warren reported three tenths of an inch of ice. Fortunately, there were no major incidents with the freezing rain and sleet such as widespread power outages and downed trees. Widespread blowing and drifting was reported with drifts in some areas a couple feet deep.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of arctic high pressure moved from the Northern Plains on on January 15th to the Upper Ohio Valley by late in the day on January 16th.  The high eventually shifted east of the area on the 17th.  Very cold temperatures accompanied this high.  The coldest temperatures since January 1994 were recorded at most observing sites.  Gusty southwest to west winds made it feel even colder with wind chills colder than minus 25 reported from the evening of the 15th through the afternoon hours of the 16th.  Winds eventually lessened early on the 17th which allowed temperatures to again plummet to 10 below or colder for the third morning in a row at many locations.  Cooperative observers in Knox County reported lows of -28 and -29 on the 17th.  Most of the schools in Northern Ohio were closed on both the 15th and 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold temperatures were reported in Lorain County on January 15th through the 17th.  Temperatures were the coldest since January of 1994.  Gusty southwest to west winds accompanied the cold temperatures and created wind chills colder than minus 30 for several hours on January 16th.  At the Lorain County Airport sustained winds were between 10 and 20 mph from the evening of the 15th through the late afternoon hours of the 16th.  Wind chills dipped below minus 30 at 3 am on the 16th and didn't climb back above minus 30 till 10 am. The coldest wind chill reported was minus 34.  Morning lows at the Lorain County Airport on the 15th through 17th were: -6, -13 and -14.  A cooperative observer near Oberlin reported morning lows on the 15th through 17th of -5, -14 and -15.  All of the schools in the county were closed on the 15th and 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked eastward from the Mississippi Valley, and moved through the Ohio Valley on the 10th. With the low tracking south of the region colder air was able to remain in place allowing for mainly snow. Snow developed during the late evening hours of the 9th across southwest portions of the forecast area and slowly progressed east through the overnight hours. The snow didn't reach far northeastern Ohio until the early morning hours of the 10th. The snow was heavy at times during the morning hours, then tapered off from west to east through mid afternoon. Accumulations ranged mainly between 6 and 12 inches with highest amounts over northwest Ohio, and the least across central Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow quickly developed during the afternoon of the 9th and visibilities dropped below 3 miles. The snow persisted through the overnight hours into the 10th, and didn't come to an end until late that evening. There was a brief period of freezing rain late on the 10th, then precipitation ended and pushed out of the area. Snowfall amounts varied between 8.0 and 14.0 inches. The Youngstown Airport measured 12.8 inches for a storm total. Trained spotters in the area measured 14.5 inches in Newton Falls, 13.5 inches in Warren, and around 9.0 inches in Mesopotamia. Many accidents were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved across the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 11th as strong low pressure moved north across the Great Lakes. A convective line along the cold front produced widespread damage across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland with thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH and a peak wind gust of 92 MPH at Allegheny County Airport near Pittsburgh. High winds gusting over 60 MPH behind the front in a strong pressure gradient produced wind damage across the entire region into midday on the 12th. In all across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland over one half million homes and businesses were without power at some point during the storm.

The northern half of the county experienced mainly snow while central and southern areas experienced some breaks in the precipitation, as well as a mix of sleet and freezing rain from time to time. Snowfall totals varied quite a bit, but even southern locations of the county were able to pick up at least 6.0 inches for the event. Trained spotters across the area measured 7.1 inches in Seville, and both Hinckley and Brunswick had 9.4 inches. Numerous accidents were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked northeast from the Tennessee Valley during the evening hours of the 27th. This area of low pressure continued northeast and moved through the Ohio River Valley, and then into western Pennsylvania during the early afternoon of the 28th. The low pressure intensified as it entered western New York State. Snow began across the region during the late afternoon hours of the 27th, and continued through the evening of the 28th. Warmer air aloft was able to effect some areas by changing precipitation over to sleet and freezing rain for a short period. Many locations across northern Ohio experienced at snowfall amounts between 8.0 and 12.0 inches.  Gusty westerly winds accompanied the snow and caused extensive blowing and drifting.  Travel was severely hampered during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the afternoon hours of the 27th and continued through the overnight and morning hours of the 28th. The snow finally tapered off during the afternoon of the 28th. However, warmer air aloft moved over the area during the early morning hours prior to sunrise allowing for snow to change over to freezing rain. The freezing rain only lasted for a few hours before changing back to snow. Also, the freezing rain affected mainly central and southern portions of the county. Northern areas were able to remain cold enough to prevent much of a changeover. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 8.0 to 13.0 inches. A trained spotters in Ashland measured 9.3 inches. Westerly winds of 15 to 25 mph with occasional gusts in excess of 30 mph accompanied the precipitation. Widespread blowing and drifting was reported with drifts in some areas a couple feet deep. Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the late afternoon hours of the 27th, and continued overnight and through the morning of the 28th. The snow finally tapered off during the late afternoon of the 28th. However, during the morning hours, the snow mixed with and changed to freezing rain and sleet across the area. The mixed precipitation lasted between five and six hours. As the low pressure pulled away from the area, colder air moved back into the area and changed any of the mixed precipitation back to snow. Snowfall accumulations were in the 4.0 to 8.0 inch range, but at least a quarter inch of ice was reported in the area during the morning hours. Fortunately, there were no major incidents with the freezing rain and sleet such as widespread power outages and downed trees.  Trained spotters measured 8.0 inches in North Industry, but only 4.5 inches in Alliance. Many accidents were reported.  Gusty westerly winds accompanied the precipitation and caused some blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked eastward from the Mississippi Valley, and moved through the Ohio Valley on the 10th. With the low tracking south of the region colder air was able to remain in place allowing for mainly snow. Snow developed during the late evening hours of the 9th across southwest portions of the forecast area and slowly progressed east through the overnight hours. The snow didn't reach far northeastern Ohio until the early morning hours of the 10th. The snow was heavy at times during the morning hours, then tapered off from west to east through mid afternoon. Accumulations ranged mainly between 6 and 12 inches with highest amounts over northwest Ohio, and the least across central Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the midday hours of the 9th and continued through the evening hours of the 10th. However, there were some significant breaks in the precipitation during the event. Snow tapered off during the evening hours of the 9th and didn't pick up again until around 6am on the 10th. Snow continued through the morning hours and was heavy at times. The snow tapered off around midday. Precipitation into the afternoon was light and actually transitioned to freezing rain later in the afternoon. The freezing rain changed back to snow before ending that evening. Snowfall amounts ranged significantly from north to south in the county. Northern areas picked up between 7.0 and 10.0 inches while the southern half ranged between 3.0 and 7.0 inches. The observers at Mansfield Lahm Airport measured 9.9 inches for the event. Other spotters in the vicinity of Mansfield measured 7.0 and 10.0 inches for the event. Many accidents were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved across the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 11th as strong low pressure moved north across the Great Lakes. A convective line along the cold front produced widespread damage across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland with thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH and a peak wind gust of 92 MPH at Allegheny County Airport near Pittsburgh. High winds gusting over 60 MPH behind the front in a strong pressure gradient produced wind damage across the entire region into midday on the 12th. In all across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland over one half million homes and businesses were without power at some point during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down in Cadiz.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms ahead of a cold front affected west central Ohio just after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A road sign was blown over.  A shed sustained minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power poles were downed along State Route 133.  One tree fell on a house, causing minor structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley through the central Great Lakes on February 11th. Showers and thunderstorms developed across the region during the evening hours of the 11th. Some of the thunderstorms became severe. Additional damage was caused by strong winds behind the cold front. Thousands of trees were downed and hundreds of homes and buildings damaged by the strong winds behind the front.  Most of the home damage was from lost shingles, but many homes also lost siding or gutters.  Even a few broken windows were reported.  A few homes also sustained significant damage from fallen trees.  Tens of thousands of people lost power during this event.  The power outages forced many school districts to delay or cancel classes on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Wood County during the evening of February 11th.  West to southwest winds gusted to more than 60 mph behind the front.  A peak gust of 62 mph was measured near Walbridge just after midnight. Winds finally diminished a couple hours before daybreak on the 12th.  Dozens of trees and a few utility poles were downed in the county causing scattered power outages.  Many buildings in the county sustained damage.  Most of this was from lost shingles, but a few buildings lost gutters or sections of siding.

Power was restored to all customers by Friday morning, February 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northerly winds blowing across Lake Erie caused intense bands of lake effect snow to develop around midnight on February 4th.  The snow bands started over Lorain County and then spread east across Cuyahoga and Medina Counties.  The snow was very fluffy and because of this, snowfall rates exceeded two inches per hour at times. Thunder and lightning was reported in Cleveland Metropolitan area during this event.  Total accumulations exceeded a foot in some locations.  The snow showers diminished during the afternoon hours.  Many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense bands of lake effect snow developed over Lorain County just before midnight on February 4th.  Most of the snow showers affected the eastern half of the county.  The snow was very fluffy with snowfall rates exceeding two inches per hour at times.  The snow showers finally tapered off during the afternoon hours.  Accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were reported over much of northern and eastern Lorain County.  A maximum of 9.9 inches was reported at Oberlin with 9.0 inches in North Ridgeville and 8.9 inches in Carlisle Township.  Many accidents and school closures were reported.

Hundreds of trees and a few utility poles were downed in the county causing scattered power outages.  Many buildings in the county sustained damage.  Most of this was from lost shingles, but a few buildings lost gutters or sections of siding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley through the central Great Lakes on February 11th. Showers and thunderstorms developed across the region during the evening hours of the 11th. Some of the thunderstorms became severe. Additional damage was caused by strong winds behind the cold front. Thousands of trees were downed and hundreds of homes and buildings damaged by the strong winds behind the front.  Most of the home damage was from lost shingles, but many homes also lost siding or gutters.  Even a few broken windows were reported.  A few homes also sustained significant damage from fallen trees.  Tens of thousands of people lost power during this event.  The power outages forced many school districts to delay or cancel classes on the 12th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved east across Richland County during the evening of February 11th.  Damaging west to southwest winds developed behind the front late in the evening.  A peak wind gust of 64 mph was measured by an automated sensor at Mansfield Lahm Airport at 1159 pm.  Earlier in the evening, a 66 mph gust was measured at the airport when a line of showers and thunderstorms moved across the county.  A gust of 63 mph was measured at 10:19 pm with a 60 mph gust measured at 11:13 pm.  Winds finally diminished toward daybreak on the 12th. Ten cars of a moving train were derailed around 11:15 pm.  This occurred about halfway between Shiloh and Shelby.  No injuries occurred.  The strong winds also overturned a semi truck on State Route 13 near Shenandoah around midnight.  A second semi truck was overturned in a parking lot in Ontario.  A lot of damage was reported across the county with hundreds of trees and power poles toppled.  Thousands of people lost power during this event.  At one time, more than 25,000 customers were without power in Richland and Ashland Counties.  Hundreds of homes in the county sustained damage.  Most of this damage was from lost shingles but many homes also lost gutters and sections of siding.  A vacant school building in Epworth lost it's entire roof.  A couple of the school districts in the county had to delay the start of school because of power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long stretch of below normal temperatures allowed a deep snowpack and significant river ice cover to develop in northern Ohio during January and early February.  Unseasonably warm weather February 7th and 8th caused a rapid melting of the snowpack, increasing streamflows and causing ice jams on many rivers tributary to Lake Erie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice Jam flooding developed along the Vermilion River near Vermilion between 9am and 4pm on February 8th.  The river ice was 8 to 14 inches thick, and jammed several times between the river gage at Mill Hollow Park and the mouth of the river.  The first ice jam activity was recorded at the river gage around 9am... with ice jam flooding beginning downstream near the town of Vermilion between 4pm and 6pm.  About 20 homes were evacuated on Riverside Drive as the water levels rose behind the ice jam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long stretch of below normal temperatures allowed a deep snowpack and significant river ice cover to develop in northern Ohio during January and early February.  Unseasonably warm weather February 7th and 8th caused a rapid melting of the snowpack, increasing streamflows and causing ice jams on many rivers tributary to Lake Erie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor ice jam flooding developed along the Chagrin River in Eastlake and the Grand River near Painesville as nearly foot thick ice jammed near the mouth of each river.  Ice along the Chagrin River backed up on the Lake Shore Boulevard bridge, flooding a local restaurant parking lot and piling ice up to four feet deep in a nearby marina.  On the Grand River, ice jams formed on several bridges in Painesville and Fairport Harbor.  The largest jam formed against a railroad bridge between the two communities, and threatened to flood homes along Hidden Harbor Drive in Fairport Harbor.  The jam remained in place for nearly two days, but released before significant property damage occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Ashtabula County during the evening of February 11th.  West to southwest winds gusted to more than 60 mph behind the front. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northerly winds blowing across Lake Erie caused intense bands of lake effect snow to develop around midnight on February 4th.  The snow bands started over Lorain County and then spread east across Cuyahoga and Medina Counties.  The snow was very fluffy and because of this, snowfall rates exceeded two inches per hour at times. Thunder and lightning was reported in Cleveland Metropolitan area during this event.  Total accumulations exceeded a foot in some locations.  The snow showers diminished during the afternoon hours.  Many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense bands of lake effect snow developed over Cuyahoga County just before midnight on February 4th.  The snow showers initially affected  the western half of the county, but quickly spread east across the remainder of the county around daybreak.  The snow was very fluffy with snowfall rates exceeding two inches per hour at times.  Thunder and lightning was reported throughout the county. The snow showers finally tapered off during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Accumulations of 6 to 10 inches were reported over much of the county.  A maximum of 14.9 inches was reported at Parma with 14.8 inches at Broadview Heights.  Spotters in Shaker Heights reported a foot of snow with 10.0 inches reported in Garfield Heights.  Officially, 11.3 inches of snow was measured at Cleveland Hopkins International Airport with most of the snow falling before noon.  Significant delays were reported at the airport.  Many accidents and school closures were reported in Cuyahoga County.

Accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were reported across the northern end of Medina County.  The greatest accumulations were in the Hinckley area.   Many accidents were reported in Medina County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak instability and moderate shear associated with a northward moving warm front allowed a favorable environment for a small line of storms to remain severe as it exited northeast Indiana into northwest Ohio. A weaker tornado developed out of one of the storms as it moved through Defiance County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service (NWS) storm survey determined that a tornado initially touched down just east of Mark Center on Williams Center-Cecil Road, where eight utility poles were blown down towards the east with one pole being snapped. Immediately to the northeast of these poles on the south side of Highway 18, a small barn was blown off its foundation, but was left intact with a farm house across the road suffering some minor roof damage. The tornado briefly lifted, but touched down once again on Openlander Road between Lockwood and State Route 18. The front porch on a home was ripped off as well as a television antenna and large tree damaged. A large barn suffered some minor roof damage. As the tornado skipped a few more times, additional trees suffered varying damage, with a few homes suffering shingle or siding damage. The total length along the broken path was roughly 5 miles with a maximum width of only around 50 yards.

Several systems moved across the area, bringing with them not only some severe weather in spots, but areas of flooding as well. Rainfall amounts through this period averaged between two and four inches in many locations. Numerous counties reported high water on roads, but much of this flooding was in areas that tend to flood easily. At least one fatality occurred as a result of the flood waters when a truck was swept off a road by the Blanchard River in Putnam County, near Glandorf. It appears the driver of the truck attempted to escape and was swept away by the flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters from the Blanchard River, near Glandorf, caused a truck to be swept off the road on Road 15, north of Road J.  Investigators believe that the driver exited the truck attempting to escape the flood water and was swept away.  The body of the 74 year old male was recovered about 900 yards from the truck a few days after flood waters receded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado briefly touched down in a field just east of Thomas Road, and just west of Mercer-Auglaize County Line Road.  The tornado was rated an EF0, with winds around 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercells developed across southwest and south central Ohio during the evening as a warm front moved across the region, producing large hail and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down in a field immediately adjacent to the NWS in Wilmington.  The tornado touched down in a field northeast of the office, coming within 1000 feet of the office.  While no condensation funnel was visible, debris and dust were kicked up in the nearby farm field with clear rotation evident to NWS meteorologists.  The tornado lifted as it approached Davids Drive, on the west side of the Wilmington airport.  The tornado was rated EF0 with maximum winds at 65 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercells developed across southwest and south central Ohio during the evening as a warm front moved across the region, producing large hail and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second tornado touched down southeast of Wilmington along Farmers Road a few minutes after the first brief touchdown near NWS Wilmington.  The extent of the damage included a barn roof partially destroyed with damage to its walls, a shed flipped and blown off its foundation, two trees uprooted and a number of large limbs downed.  Metal siding was ripped off the barn roof and thrown 20 yards. This tornado was also rated an EF0 with maximum winds at 65 to 78 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercells developed across southwest and south central Ohio during the evening as a warm front moved across the region, producing large hail and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down over the southwest portion of Bethel, near Fossyl Drive and the baseball diamonds at Burke Park.  The tornado moved east-southeast and downed a tree limb on South Ash Street.  It lifted shortly thereafter as it passed just south of downtown Bethel.  The tornado was rated an EF0 with maximum winds at 65 to 70 mph.

Thunderstorms affected western Ohio during the evening of the 13th and again early on the 14th ahead of a cold front.  A localized area of high winds occurred near Wilmington on the evening of the 13th in the wake of scattered thunderstorms.  Wind gusts near 60 mph were recorded outside of thunderstorms for a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph gust was recorded at NWS Wilmington.  A 56 mph gust also occurred at the Wilmington ASOS (ILN).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough and low pressure moving across the Ohio Valley pushed warm and humid air northward ahead of a cold front moving east with the surface low. Severe thunderstorms were widespread across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Significant flash flooding occurred over eastern Allegheny and western Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania with estimated damage of 18 million dollars to public infrastructure and private buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees and power lines down near Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across Ohio during the morning of June 1st.  Hail was the main threat with these storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled over the Ohio Valley and combined with an upper level short wave to produce damaging storms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A convergent path extending about 50 yards was found in a wheat field. No structural damage was reported. Event occurred 3 miles south-southeast of Amanda, Ohio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across northern Ohio during the afternoon hours of May 27th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed along this front.  A few of the thunderstorms became severe and locally heavy rainfall was also reported.  Flash flooding occurred in Ottawa and Sandusky Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving, training thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall in Port Clinton, flooding nearly every street in town.  Basement flooding was common due to the high water in the streets.  The hospital lobby experienced flooding starting around 2:33pm local time, with water one to two feet deep just outside the main entrance.  The foundation of a bank building at East 2nd and Madison collapsed around 3:55pm.  3.75 inches of rain fell in one hour at the sewage treatment plant between 1:30pm and 2:30pm.  Storm total rainfall of 5.1 inches was measured in Port Clinton, with 4.5 inches measured on Harbor Road just east of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across northern Ohio on May 28th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of this front.  At least one thunderstorm became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across northern Ohio on May 28th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of this front.  At least one thunderstorm became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front remained nearly stationary across northern Ohio on the afternoon of May 26th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed over the area in association with this front.  A couple of the thunderstorms became severe.  Locally heavy rainfall and flash flooding were reported in Huron County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front remained nearly stationary across northern Ohio on the afternoon of May 26th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed over the area in association with this front.  A couple of the thunderstorms became severe.  Locally heavy rainfall and flash flooding were reported in Huron County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall formed over southeastern Huron County during the afternoon of the 26th.  Rainfall of 4 to 5 inches fell between Greenwich, Fitchville, and New London between approximately 3pm and 5:30pm local time.  A rain gage in Fitchville measured 5.1 inches during the event.  Several roads in the area were flooded, including SR 13 at the Southwest Branch Vermilion River, SR 162 between New London and Fitchville, and US 250 near Exchange Road southeast of Fitchville.  Many basements in the area were flooded for the first time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across northern Ohio on May 28th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of this front.  At least one thunderstorm became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm downburst caused damage in Elyria.  Winds estimated to be at least 60 mph tore a large section of roof off of a church.  Several trees were knocked near the church as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary ran from central Indiana, on southeast, to near the Ironton vicinity of extreme southern Ohio. Clusters of showers and thunderstorms rode southeast along this boundary.  The low level moisture inflow from the southwest was able to enhance the rain over portions of Lawrence County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of around 2 inches in 2 hours caused small streams to flood roads.  Streams such as Little Pine, Ellisonville, Little Storms, and Cannons Creeks flooded.  Around a half dozen homes were evacuated. Around 8 homes sustained minor to major damage, while 14 other properties were affected to a lesser degree.  One resident in a mobile home had his car and ATV flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across northern Ohio during the afternoon hours of May 27th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed along this front.  A few of the thunderstorms became severe and locally heavy rainfall was also reported.  Flash flooding occurred in Ottawa and Sandusky Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving training thunderstorms developed across central and eastern portions of Sandusky County between 330pm and 5pm local time.  Torrential rainfall of up to 4.5 inches in two hours caused widespread flash flooding in Fremont.  Flooding was reported in both the Sheriff Department and Health Department buildings.  Many commercial and residential basements in Fremont flooded.  High water stranded cars on roadways throughout town, including  portions of US Route 20 and State Route 53.  Flash Flooding was also reported along Muskellunge Creek just west of Fremont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across northeastern Ohio on the evening of March 28th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed along this front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree southeast of Ravenna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled over the Ohio Valley and combined with an upper level short wave to produce damaging storms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred three miles northwest of Darbyville, near the intersection of Darby Creek and London Road. Three barns and a home had significant damage, and a trailer was moved a short distance to the southeast. A short distance from here had another two homes damaged, along with several trees snapped. The microburst was .4 miles long and up to 350 yards wide. One man was injured when the tractor he was on tipped over.

An area of low pressure tracked through Northern Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of the 9th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across north-central and northeast Ohio during the late afternoon and evening hours. Many of the thunderstorms became severe. A tornado was also confirmed in Wayne County Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down on Wenger Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked through Northern Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of the 9th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: EF0 tornado was confirmed by NWS storm survey.  The tornado followed an intermittent path approximately 4 miles long just west of Smithville.  Over two dozen trees were snapped along the path of the storm.  The tornado destroyed a garden shed on Honeytown Road, causing damage to several trees and scattering debris up to a mile away.  The tornado left a well defined path through an adjacent potato field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved southeast through northern Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of the 19th. The thunderstorms tracked along a boundary that stretched across the southern Great Lakes and into the central Appalacians. The thunderstorms became severe across north-central Ohio. Later into the overnight hours of the 20th, a cold front approached western Ohio where additional showers and thunderstorms moved into the area. Some of the storms became severe across Northwest Ohio. Showers and thunderstorms continued east across northern Ohio during the overnight, but there were no severe reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms fired across southeast Ohio during the heat and instability of the afternoon.  The area was well south of an east to west front.  The storms became multicellular and formed short lines. Numerous outflow boundaries were produced.||Large hail was noted quickly, with strong surface wind gusts developing as the episode unfolded.  The heaviest downpours were noted in southern Washington County, causing street flooding in the city of Marietta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary along the Ohio River provided a focus for thunderstorm activity ahead of a cold front. This translated to a weak trough axis that extended from northern Kentucky to central Ohio later in the day, also providing a focus for strong storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were also reported down from the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved southeast through northern Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of the 19th. The thunderstorms tracked along a boundary that stretched across the southern Great Lakes and into the central Appalacians. The thunderstorms became severe across north-central Ohio. Later into the overnight hours of the 20th, a cold front approached western Ohio where additional showers and thunderstorms moved into the area. Some of the storms became severe across Northwest Ohio. Showers and thunderstorms continued east across northern Ohio during the overnight, but there were no severe reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down.

Thunderstorms fired across southeast Ohio during the heat and instability of the afternoon.  The area was well south of an east to west front.  The storms became multicellular and formed short lines. A large thunderstorm complex moved southeast into Athens County after dawn on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain amounts of 2 to 2.5 inches fell in about 2 hours. A spotter reported 1.6 inches in an hour.  The 24 hour rain total was 3.5 inches at Nelsonville.  The town of Athens was just south of the heaviest rain with a 24 hour rain total of 1.8 inches.  Small streams flooded and closed several roads.  The stream gauge at Doanville on the lower reaches of Monday Creek rose 10 to 11 feet in a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley during the 28th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved through the region. Some of the storms became severe during the afternoon hours. A line of showers and thunderstorms moved through much of the area during the evening hours, but there were no reports of severe winds or hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed in eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania ahead of a cold front moving across the Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported power lines down in Uhrichsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving line of storms moved out of northern Indiana and into northwestern Ohio. While the storms had weakened prior to their arrival in Ohio, training of a few of the storms caused areas of flooding and flash flooding across portions of southern Williams county and northern Defiance county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported several roads with water flowing on them, some a foot deep in areas, as thunderstorms dropped two to four inches of rain over a short period of time. These roads included State Route 576, west of Bryan, US 127 between Byran and West unity and State Route 34, east of Stryker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of thunderstorms moved through during the afternoon hours sparking straight line damaging winds, large hail, flash flooding, and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service confirmed straight-line wind damage off of State Route 772 in Buchanan.  Three mobile homes were knocked off their foundation, picked up, lifted 20 yards, and were completely destroyed.  The roof of one mobile home was carried approximately 50 yards in the same direction.  Two additional mobile homes remained on their foundation and sustained minor damage.  Approximately five trees were also uprooted.  Damage in this case was determined to be straight-line because there were indications that all of the structures were blown consistently to the southeast.  The mobile homes were subject to high winds because they were located at the summit of a hill in an open field.  This allowed severe thunderstorm winds to lift the mobile homes off their foundations.  Five people in mobile homes were injured when their mobile homes were knocked off of their foundations.  The five people were taken to the hospital with non-life threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked across the region along a cold front during the evening hours of June 1st. The cold front stretched from the Northern Great Lakes to the southern Plains. Some of the thunderstorms became severe during the evening hours. The activity pushed off to the east and diminished shortly before midnight on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across northern Ohio early on July 31st.  Thunderstorms developed in advance of the front.  One of the thunderstorms produced a weak tornado in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in rural Wayne County about five miles south of downtown Wooster.  The tornado was rated an EF1 and was on the ground for around a quarter mile.  The damage path was less than 20 yards in width.  The tornado touched down about a mile east of State Route 83 on the north side of East Messner Road and continued east for a couple hundred yards before lifting.  A pole barn was damaged and portions of a fence knocked down by the tornado.  Several trees were toppled along the damage path.  Sheet metal from the barn was found as much as a mile and a half away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong post-frontal gradient winds developed on December 9th, strongest during the late morning and afternoon as a deep area of low pressure tracked into northern Lower Michigan. Widespread wind gusts between 45 and 60 mph were common across much of northwest Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very windy conditions were seen across the area on December 9th as a deep area of low pressure tracked into Michigan.  Wind gusts between 45 and 60 mph were common during the late morning and afternoon hours.  There were reports of power lines and trees down across the region due to the strong winds, which resulted in isolated power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved across the Central Great Lakes early on October 7th.  A cold front trailing this low swept across northern Ohio.  Strong winds behind the front downed a few trees and power lines.  Most of this damage occurred in northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported across inland Ashtabula County.  Peak wind gusts were estimated to be around 45 mph.  A few trees and power lines were downed resulting in a couple power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong center of low pressure tracked out of the plains states to the Great Lakes region. Ahead of this low in the Ohio Valley, southwest winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 and 60 mph were common throughout the day. These strong winds peaked in the early afternoon with the passage of a cold front, and diminished later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down across the county. A silo was blown over in Cherry Fork.
