EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked through Northern Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of the 9th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across north-central and northeast Ohio during the late afternoon and evening hours. Many of the thunderstorms became severe. A tornado was also confirmed in Wayne County Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure located over eastern Canada was nearly stationary during this time. During the afternoon of the 25th, a trough of low pressure swung south across the Great Lakes region. This feature combined with warm and humid air at the surface caused showers and thunderstorms to develop by mid afternoon. Thunderstorms continued across the entire region during the evening hours, with many becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many large limbs were reported down.

A warm and unstable airmass caused showers and thunderstorms to develop shortly after noontime and persist through the afternoon and evening hours.  Some of the thunderstorms became severe producing severe winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three people were struck by lightning along Elizabeth Township Route 323.  Four people were riding all terrain vehicles, when a downpour had them seek shelter under a tree.  One of the victims said, there was no thunder or lightning. We were just trying to stay dry and the lightning just hit all at once.  One woman was knocked unconscious for about 20 minutes. The 2 females were transported by ambulance to Cabell Huntington Hospital, while the third person did not seek treatment.   The woman that was knocked unconscious checked out of the hospital with no injuries.  The other woman spent 3 days in the hospital undergoing tests.   She was still having vision problems and pain in her hands and feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain amounts of 1.5 to 2 inches fell in an hour, causing poor drainage areas to overflow and block several roads.  Urban street flooding was also common for a brief time, when storm drains could not handle the runoff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked eastward across the northern Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening of the 20th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front and tracked eastward across the area. Many of the thunderstorms became severe in the very warm and humid airmass in place across the region. The thunderstorms diminished during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs and power lines were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved east across northern Ohio during the predawn hours of September 28th.  Showers and thunderstorms occurred ahead of the front.  At least one of the thunderstorms became severe.  Strong and gusty winds continued behind the front through the evening hours.  Peak measured wind gusts at the automated wind sensors in northern Ohio ranged from 40 to 48 mph.  A few trees were knocked down by the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest winds were reported in Lake County on September 28th.  Peak gusts were around 45 mph.  An automated sensor in downtown Cleveland measured a peak gust of 48 mph.  A few trees were downed by the strong winds in Mentor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thin line of storms produced isolated severe weather over portions of Ohio during the late afternoon and early evening hours of October 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on reports from the public and from a storm survey conducted by National Weather service personnel, it was determined that a weak tornado touched down briefly in Clinton County.  The tornado touched down in a shallow ravine near the intersection of State Route 134 and Horseshoe Road where it knocked down a few small trees and large branches.  In addition, two small windows were broken on a barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms dumped 1.5 to 2 inches of rain between 0000E and 0800E.  Due to previous rains over the past week, runoff was efficient.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Creeks such as Ice and Big Branch overflowed into the usual low spots. Backflow through the sewer system and urban street flooding caused water to get inside numerous basements in the city of Ironton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe weather formed during the late afternoon and early evening hours across portions of Kentucky and Ohio. The main threat was damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines were reported down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of July 21st.  A few of the stronger storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail the size of half dollars was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of showers and thunderstorms brought flash flooding and flooding to portions of Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over roads and some basements were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center tracked from Missouri to Michigan.  Meanwhile, its associated upper level trough pushed a cold front through southeast Ohio just before sunset.  A fast moving band of rain, along and immediately ahead of the front, featured a narrow line of embedded showers.  These convective showers helped mix down the winds that were located at 4 to 6 thousand feet above the ground.  Surface wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were common.||Later that night, wind gusts near 60 mph occurred, as the colder air poured in.||Power outages were common throughout southeast Ohio, as tree branches fell onto power lines.  One major utility company reported the electricity remained out until late on the 13th or early on the 14th for some of its customers in southeast Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms sparked ahead of a cold front during the afternoon on Monday June 8th, with more widespread storms occurring overnight with the actual frontal passage.

A deep upper trough and low pressure moving across the Ohio Valley pushed warm and humid air northward ahead of a cold front moving east with the surface low. Severe thunderstorms were widespread across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Significant flash flooding occurred over eastern Allegheny and western Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania with estimated damage of 18 million dollars to public infrastructure and private buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported road flooding due to flash flooding north of Roswell.
